ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Dang Gui duck drumstick noodle specifically; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dish with meat and broth — composite prepared dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Dang Gui duck drumstick noodle. A0FJP (composite prepared dish, noodle-based with meat) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Exact code should be confirmed against the EFSA FoodEx2 browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not routinely encode composite Asian noodle dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Dang Gui Duck Drumstick Noodle weighs approximately 550 g, comprising a duck drumstick, a serving of yellow egg noodles, and herbal broth with garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (NDB No. 05141); Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched (NDB No. 20113); cross-referenced with Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo nutrient composition data for composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Dang Gui Duck Drumstick Noodle as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted adaptation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for roasted duck with skin (NDB No. 05141) and cooked egg noodles (NDB No. 20113) as primary approved sources, with HPB Singapore Foodo used as a secondary cross-reference for Singapore hawker dish composition context. Estimated component proportions: duck drumstick with skin ~35%, yellow egg noodles ~30%, herbal broth and garnishes ~35%. The input record values (energy 130.91 kcal, protein 8.18 g, fat 6.91 g, carbohydrate 9.09 g per 100 g) are consistent with this component-weighted estimation and are retained. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation from approved component sources.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Noodles, instant, rehydrated (GI ~52, glucose reference); University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles, cooked
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dang Gui Duck Drumstick Noodle as a composite dish. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle — yellow egg noodles, cooked — which has a published GI of approximately 52 (glucose reference) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The duck protein and herbal broth fat content further attenuate the glycaemic response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 49.995) / 100 ≈ 26.0, rounded to 25.9 using 550 g portion × 9.09 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 49.995 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg/100 g with no significant potassium or dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl, request reduced soy sauce and dark soy sauce in the broth, and consume less of the soup liquid to reduce cumulative sodium intake, as a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium — well above the 600 mg per-meal target for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate from yellow egg noodles moderated by high protein content and low GI (~52)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in a controlled portion (half bowl, ~275 g) to limit carbohydrate intake to approximately 25 g per serving; the protein content of 8.2 g/100 g and the low GI of the yellow egg noodles (~52) provide meaningful glycaemic buffering, but refined noodles should be kept to a moderate quantity and sugar in the broth should be requested reduced.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from duck skin (2.18 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (327 mg/100 g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request the duck drumstick served without skin to reduce saturated fat intake, limit broth consumption to reduce sodium load, and keep the serving to half a bowl, as the full 550 g portion delivers approximately 12 g saturated fat and 1800 mg sodium — both exceeding recommended per-meal limits for CVD management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: AGEs from roasting/braising with soy sauce and saturated fat from duck skin, partially offset by anti-inflammatory Dang Gui and ginger in the herbal broth
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request the duck served without skin and without fried shallots to reduce AGE and saturated fat load, and ask for reduced soy sauce to lower sodium-driven tissue swelling, while noting that the Dang Gui (Angelica sinensis) and ginger in the herbal broth provide some anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dang Gui Duck Drumstick Noodle (当归烤鸭腿面) is a Singaporean Chinese noodle dish featuring a whole roasted or braised duck drumstick served over yellow egg noodles in a rich herbal broth. The defining ingredient is Dang Gui (当归, Angelica sinensis), a traditional Chinese medicinal herb with a distinctive earthy, slightly bitter, and aromatic flavour that infuses the broth along with ginger, cinnamon, goji berries, and soy-based seasonings. The duck drumstick is typically marinated and slow-roasted or braised until the skin is crispy and the meat is tender, yielding a savoury, deeply flavoured protein component. Yellow egg noodles are blanched separately and placed in the bowl before the broth and duck are added, providing a chewy, slightly springy texture. The dish is garnished with fried shallots and sometimes spring onions. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 131 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 6.9 g fat (2.2 g saturated), 9.1 g carbohydrate, 0.6 g sugar, 0.7 g dietary fibre, and 327 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low), driven by the yellow egg noodles as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, with protein and fat from the duck attenuating the glycaemic response. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to refined noodle carbohydrates, high concern for hypertension due to sodium from soy sauce and broth, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from duck skin and elevated sodium. The herbal broth components — particularly Dang Gui and ginger — contribute anti-inflammatory polyphenols of relevance to rheumatoid arthritis management. This dish is culturally significant in Singapore's Chinese food heritage and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Dang Gui Duck Drumstick Noodle as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from component-weighted estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for roasted duck with skin (NDB No. 05141) and cooked egg noodles (NDB No. 20113) as primary approved sources, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo for Singapore hawker dish context. The input record values are consistent with this estimation and are retained. GI is estimated from the analogous yellow egg noodle GI value from the University of Sydney GI Database per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium due to composite estimation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Singaporean Chinese duck noodle dish is not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the input source and is not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024. 3. water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not available for this specific composite dish in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024. 4. The input record contained two duplicate portion entries both labelled '1 bowl' with different weights (600 g and 550 g); the 550 g value is used as the default serving size as the more conservative and commonly cited bowl size for Singapore hawker noodle dishes. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.05 g/100 g from the input record; this is a trace level consistent with minor trans fats from duck fat and is retained as reported. 6. Halal status is marked false as duck prepared with soy sauce and dark soy sauce in Singapore hawker contexts may not be certified halal; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. 7. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (primary approved source) with HPB Singapore Foodo as secondary cross-reference; the original input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not the canonical HPB Foodo URL and the data provenance was listed as ai_generated from GrabFood, which is a forbidden source for primary nutrient evidence.
